Yeah, for sure.
Share the context ahead of time.

I'm relatively familiar with Arkose.

It was in the bot mitigation space in the early days.
so, yeah, somewhat familiar.
But I love those folks over at Webstacks.
I appreciate them referring us.
Quick on me.

I joined back in October.
Previous to that, actually, a little over a year ago, I actually was the first GrowthX customer.
Most of my career is in digital and growth.
I spent 19 years there before moving on to GrowthX.

A lot of my experience lies in working with brands that are kind of stuck paying too much for paid search, need to diversify their channels, help...usually, they're hiring me to help them find new paid channels, as well as build an organic engine.
Most recently, I was at a company called Homebase, where I was doing that, but a variety of different stops, like Distilled Networks, as I mentioned, Trade.io, Anaplan, Zendoso.
And, yeah, you know, that's...was...it was a big motivator in me bringing on GrowthX.
I was basically...I had a big team.
I was coordinating different things around and scaling a lot of content.
And...
It was quite expensive and just took a lot of my time and I had to do many, many other things at Fathombase.

And I met Marcel, our CEO and founder, when he was doing some workshops last year, just sharing some cool things he built, leveraging AI workflows and figuring out, really experimenting of, like, what is that right human in the loop layer and how do you get to some sort of repeatability with quality content.
And that's kind of what brought me to bring him on at GrubbX, or at Homebase, rather, and then eventually just being excited about the GrubbX opportunity, joined with Tom's jumping in here.
That's what brought me here as well.
So, yeah, happy to share a bit more, of course, and how we work and things of that nature.

Marisol, you want to talk about how you found WebStacks?
Yes, of course.
So we are looking for a vendor that can help us work with our website to rebuild it and make it scalable and fast.
And I arrived at WebStacks thanks to ChatGPT.
And when I got there, they had like this message that it said, did you arrive here because of ChatGPT?
So I said yes.
And then we booked a call with them to chat about that.
We also have another call that is about the website.
But they had a call with us and they explained to us what they're doing and things like that.
So that's why they suggested to talk to you guys.
Got it.
So just to make sure I'm clear on the need.
So it sounds like you're talking with WebStacks.
Webstacks about helping manage your website.
And then did we come up through just varying content needs?
Right.
That's correct.
So we also wanted to talk to them and see we're in the process of doing a vendor selection around GEO, right?
So SEO and then GEO.
And so we wanted to talk to them and see what their capabilities were around that.
But they were like, well, that's not sort of our thing.
We help and support that.
But that's not specifically what they do.
And so and then they were like, well, you know, a lot of this is content.
And some of our customers work with GrowthX and we've had a really good experience with them.
So you might want to talk to them, too.
And so what we're looking at is we want to talk to y'all about, you know, what y'all learned about, you know, content and GEO and then.
you.

How y'all work with customers to create content that is meaningful for customers, meaningful for prospects, and is still SEO, GEO, you know, not high ranking for SEO, but also, you know, included in the right prompts for GEO.
Super helpful.
Yeah, happy to share a bit more.
And Tom, Kyle, Tom is our interim CMO.
Oh, okay.
Great to meet you, Tom.
Cool.

Well, you know, from my perspective, you know, how I think about SEO and GEO, how we think about it here, is generally our hypothesis is if we go and we apply the right rigor to really understanding your business at a really deep level, really starting to dig into your personas, their jobs to be done, their pain points, challenges, what excites them, etc.
There's naturally a variety of different questions.
They were going to have associated to that.
And even before GEO became prominent, that was a big part of our methodology of really trying to understand how can we create amazing answers to those questions.
I will say even before, you know, growthx, that generally was a shared philosophy in how I would think about SEO and Marisol.
And that was one of the reasons I started to work with him because that really resonated with me.
You know, there at times has been a bit of like a stigma historically of like SEO content and it being less than and just like this is obvious SEO.
Right.
generally our mindset has been, you know, that tends to be a bit too short term focused.
If you think about SEO content in that way, you need to create content that is truly going to engage your audience.
And still need them to come back, right?
Yeah, exactly.
I totally agree with you.
Yeah.
And so what I don't want to undersell that there are particular things that you can optimize for for GEO.

You know, there are certainly some things that seem to show that if you approach the right, you know, do varying things with schema, if you have, you know, enough grand mentions that are out there, that there does appear to be some correlation to strength in GEO.
But, you know, at the end of the day, there's also a lot of complexity of, like, measuring, are you gaining traction there?
We partner with Scrunch.
We work with a lot of our customers to identify varying prompts and competitors that we need to track.
So we kind of, we view that as, like, that's table stakes for any content program at this point.

We are still doing a lot of the different things of how are we measuring traction for your SEO program, but also how can we understand, are you actually, you know, are you actually gaining momentum in generative?
Engines, well as what kind of just like key learnings can be extracted from that, where your competitors may have some traction and that you don't, and does that inform the content that we create?
Happy to share particularly how we go about that, but just addressing that at a high level, that's how we think about it.
So what is Scrunch?

So there's a variety of different, you may have come across some of these just in your own research of different tools that are, have spun up quite quickly just to track prompts in these different engines, Perplexity, ChatGPT, etc.
I'm happy to show you like very quickly, I don't want to get us like too in the weeds before like talking a bit more about GrowthX, but here's like a high level of like how that works.
So this is, this is for one brand we work with in the security space called Abnormal Security, actually Abnormal AI now, we are tracking a variety of different prompts.
In this case, 88 prompts for them.
And basically, you just set these up.
You can categorize them.
You get to get a view of your presence versus competitors.
We're still pretty early on in the engagement with them.
And so working through varying things of like, how can we learn from this and understand, are there certain trends their competitors have more traction on than they do?
And what can we apply from that?
And certainly, that has been one of the early takeaways for them.
And so there's, I won't go into do this like full like breakdown of just scrunch because this is only like a sliver of everything that we do here.
But just sharing since GEO is top of mind, we do.
We know abnormal security very well, very well.
Are you working with any other security, cybersecurity companies?
We are.
Well, obviously, that can be quite broad, but 701, Okta, abnormal, I feel like I'm forgetting.
But those are a few that come to mind initially.
Are you working with any of your competitors?
Are you working with Fingerprint, JS, Imperba?
No, no.
That I am quite certain of.
Just again, my familiarity with the space back when I was at Distil Networks.
absolutely.
So how would this work, right?
You come in and, you know, you work with us to get some, you know, included in the GEO prompts.
But do you also not only looking at sort of the measurement, but do you also look, do you also help with the content and creating the content?
Yeah, that's really our specialty is, and happy to share a little bit more about that.

But essentially, you know, where our company was born was a lot of former in-house marketers that had a lot of success in terms of building organic content engines and then started to really do a lot of experiments.
of how can we help these teams move a lot faster and focus on other things by bringing AI into the mix.
So essentially, we have created an AI workflow platform that enables our internal team.
And so essentially, we are, you know, this hybrid of platform and service.

On the services side, we have a variety of different folks from, you know, strategy to managing editors, the people who are, like, hands-on in content that are your day-to-day points of contact, meeting with you weekly, collaborating on Slack, Notion, et cetera.
Those are the folks who are really just, like, getting in super deep and understanding and developing a strategy with you.
And then they are armed by these AI workflows that enable them to go very quick.

And a lot of what we have figured out is, I shouldn't say figured out because it is certainly always evolving, that we feel that we have momentum compared to what we've seen others do in this space, put it that way, is...
What are those necessary inputs, process, human-in-the-loop steps to get more repeatable outcomes from AI content?
And when you're able to dial that in, it just really can change the trajectory of, like, how fast you can move, the cost of that, how quick you can validate hypotheses, et cetera.
And so I'm happy to share a little bit of, like, how we think about that and some of the learnings and process.
But from a high level, that is how we operate.
You were saying that y'all are still fresh and new with Abnormal.
How long have y'all been working with them?
I think I want to say it was February, but my memory is not what it once used to be.
Earlier this year, but, you know, happy to, if there is, you know, a fit here and eventually you want to talk to the varying folks.
Abdallah is someone who meets their digital over there we know quite well.
very Thank Thank Thank you.
I'm to connect you with them.
But that's a case where they have a...
y'all seeing measurable results?
I mean, because that's kind of a...
I mean, if it says February, that's almost six months, right?
Oh, no, certainly.
You know, most of our accounts, like by month three is when you're starting to see, like, the first notable uptick in traffic.
Not that you're not seeing signal before, but usually pretty consistently around month three, when you really start to publish a higher volume.
Or in their case, they actually already have a lot of content.
So it's a bit of part refresh, part helping new lanes for them.
That's what it would be for us is refresh.
Yeah.
And so...
But I can share a little bit more of, like, how we operate and how we identify some of these opportunities and how we've, you know, been able to find success.
So, you know, happy to just answer any follow-up questions from here, but just to give you a little bit...
More context on like how we think and operate.
So for us, you know, I would get like a cough drop.
Hold on just a second.
Yeah, no worries.
I'll give you just a moment.
And before we move on, Kyle, it's real important for us to be able to link these activities to conversions, right?
They're not just showing up.
So we'd like to hear about y'all's capabilities.
I think my gen part of it, because that's super important.
Yeah, yeah, no, absolutely.
I mean, we don't have a single customer that comes to us that would be satisfied if we weren't connecting to conversion at some point in time.
I think the key is, though, making sure you have the right foundation to ultimately.
And so, you know, the way we like to think about it is there's a variety of different ways we can apply our methodology, leverage AI to, you know, effective varying aspects of the growth funnel.
But really, our aspiration is for our customers to think and operate more like publishers and put out high-quality content at scale.
And then as we get more eyeballs, that naturally opens up more paths for distribution and conversion.
What we find is a lot of times it's pretty rare where our customers have that content strategy dialed in across the board and so need some help in really starting to dig in there.
And what we find is starting with what we call editorial content and as well as exploring content refreshes, to your point, that tends to be longer-form content.
This could be blogs, but not limited to blogs.
There's just so much important coordination that goes into dialing in voice, tone, structure, topics that are resonating with your audience.
Are they actually resonating with the right people that can continue just to grow and evolve that side and find opportunity?
But naturally, what we find is that also tends to open up opportunities for maybe programmatic.
That's like more template-based content that can scale, can open up paths for lead magnets, for email.
We've done a variety of different experimentation with outbound.

And the point being here is we certainly have capabilities to do a lot of different things, but we like to start at editorial content just to make sure we have that right context and strategy first and foremost.
It's not that we won't start with those other things.
It's just we tend to be quite selective with Okta.
Like we're doing a very custom engagement.
We're not even doing editorial content.
We're specifically helping them run experiments on their outbound motion.
For Udemy, we're helping stamp the newsletter.
But, you know, those tend to be more exceptions.

We have a highly editorially focused team.

You know, we have that managing director strategy layer I mentioned, folks with like 20-plus years in growth and SEO experience from Fanatics, ClickUp, just like, you know, really, really massive content engines.
And then we have a layer of managing editors, and this is the folks that are really hands-on with their content, getting feedback, et cetera.
That's quite common in like editorially publisher focused teams.
Sydney was the lead editor at Sunrush, for example, and so this is someone who, you know, would be really hands-on with like shaping your content, ensuring that it's a high quality.
just for time's sake, just diving in a little bit because I think this
It can help show a little bit of how we think and operate and to really start to get to a point of higher quality content and how we've been able to have success with AI.
Essentially, what we start out with with our engagements is what we call a strategy sprint.

This is a six to eight week process where we are diving into a variety of different opportunities, really developing a lot of deep insights and deliverables, essentially creating a lot of AI artifacts, a roadmap, and then calibrating on content with you and starting to create and scale that content.

Essentially, this is a key part to what we have been able to find success with AI content is creating these different artifacts that you can then are really useful for us to get feedback from you and can also be broken up and leveraged with our workflows.
So essentially, you know, with any engagement, of course, starting out with the kickoff and then synthesizing what we're hearing.
And getting feedback from you, et cetera.
Then we start to create some, you know, first briefs and understand, like, are we thinking about you in the right way and getting context from you?
Like, are we missing anything that is critical to speaking to your customers and whatever that may be?
This is something that powers a bit of our research workflows amongst other things.
But then we drill in quite a bit deeper on the persona side.
You know, typically play around with a variety of different archetypes.
This is where, you know, we really start to drill in deeper of, like, who are these people, what motivates them, how do we win their trust, lose their trust, et cetera.
From there, we start to really make sure you have refined writing guidelines.
You probably already have some version of this, but what we find is there's usually some room for improvement for a lot of our customers as well as getting that to be AI ready.
What we find, for example, is giving good examples and bad examples tends to work quite well.
So across a variety of different things, around voice, tone, structure, et cetera.
won't go through all this, but just like point being is like quite a bit of depth in terms of setting up these artifacts and input.
It's not just us simply giving high-level context to a workflow and then a keyword and then just creating content.
It is things like this that helps to shape and get more consistent results.
From there, what we do is we start to calibrate on all of this and start to actually apply it to your content.

From here, what we found is really useful is starting to look at an existing piece of content, rewrite it based off of this context, and then break it up in a chance of just here's what you said, here's how we rewrite it, here's why.
We just find this gives us just all this high-level part of the process, and there's a lot more other things that I won't go into for time's sake.
It's helped us start to identify different patterns, and this is all the type of things that enable us.
To start to play around with different inputs into our workflows.
as we can start to understand those patterns better, it enables us to move all the much faster and get to a consistent bar of quality content.
So this is a little bit of what happens in like the shaping phase and creating the artifacts.
I can share a bit of what that looks like in our platform.
And again, like this is where our team is, this platform largely first focused on enabling our team.
But there is a day in the not too distant future.
We will start to open this up a bit for our customers and being able to run workflows and see what we're doing.
But here's where things like those artifacts come into place.
And think of these as like living documents that as your business evolves or, you know, this engagement evolves, we can continue to come back.
And these can be then referenced into our workflows.
We can create a variety of different things in terms of rules and things of that nature.
But the key here where the team spending a lot.
All of their time as well is we create these different pipelines for you.
And so that could be, you know, this is built to focus on refreshing content.
This is for this customer specifically about a particular programmatic SEO opportunity.
And in this case, this example here is really about creating editorially focused content.
And so essentially you have a variety of different topics here and think of these columns as different workflows built to do the multiple steps of generating this piece of content.
so this is where like a managing editor would be spending most of their time.
And essentially what we have here is each one of these has varying inputs and outputs as any workflow would.
So a person can go through and pull in those artifacts, set the keyword, as well as just do different things to kind of shape the initial brief that we're creating.
From there, running this.
It's going to pull in data from sources like SEMrush, Perplexity, ChatGPT, to just create this initial high-level brief, creating an assignment, if you will.
And this is just where managing it and having that context on you can first start to shape and understand, like, does this make sense?

Does this have – essentially, we like to think of it as, like, enabling people who have really great taste and start to continue to play around with and modify the different inputs and outputs that we see from these workflows.
What's happening in the background is quite a powerful workflow, Jean.
So, Kyle, so we only have, like, about five, four, six minutes left.
And so, I think that – I mean, this is a lot of great detail in the workflow and everything.
But how do you – when you – from this stage, right, and once we get, like, the content refreshed and it's structured on our website appropriately and it's been, you know –
Edited, you know, for, for, um, GEO, you know, do you have any examples of how you're connecting, you know, the GEO exercise to conversions?
Like, how do you do that?

Um, well, I think the key to keep in mind when it comes to that side of things is, um, the how we do that at the end of the day, we can be a partner and we all have a lot of experience in building that variety of stops, but ultimately that's going to be owned by our customers.
Um, we're not going to create, um, you know, attribution systems for you and things of that nature, but we can talk about how we would, uh, think about the measurement of this.
And some of the complexities of measuring a GEO in terms of down funnel, for example, is you essentially have kind of two scenarios, right?
You have, um, your, um, you are referenced, uh, and there is a direct link and someone clicks that link, uh, to your website.
And then eventually actions happen either in that session or a feature session in Convert, right?
By influence.
Yeah, there's different ways you can start to measure that, of course, right?
That is, well, that happens and we track that in our dashboards for our customers and we go book our studio dashboards and we can look at GA and look at that refer data and then measure off of that.
That is only going to be a subset of the influence that's happening here.

And so this is actually what I would say is a interesting challenge for a lot of marketers today in really starting to have to think about their ways of measurement and like what channels you're going to be looking at.
So, like, for example, let's say we set you up and we have a good system for tracking prompts.
We feel like we have a good pulse that you are you are having growing momentum in Geo.
At that point, we need to understand where would you see that?
Are you seeing that in branded search?
Are you seeing that in direct traffic visits, et cetera?
And then it starts to.
Bring into, like, well, how would you measure that?
And how I've done this sorts of stuff in the past pre-GEO is think about things like incrementality.
And so can I get a good sense of my baseline and then understand if I'm confident my baseline is here and that we have momentum in GEO?
And that is the variable.
Am I seeing growth in branded search and direct traffic?
And it is not a perfect science, but nor is any attribution system.
So it's an interesting complexity that a lot of brands are facing now of, like, how to think about GEO.
And, you know, I'd say some marketers have had to harden this kind of practice of thinking about incrementality, maybe MMM, although MMM in this case is probably overkill.
So, you know, it is a phase where we can help support and then help, you know, educate and enable in a variety of ways.
But there's naturally going to be some line of, like, we're also not going to, like, get into your, you know, HubSpot or whatever.
Use and set up your system.
Yeah, no, but would you be able to help us set up GA4 events?
I mean, would that help?
Typically, that would be something that we wouldn't normally do, but also, like, it sometimes can be quite simple.
And the WebStacks team also is, like, should be well-enabled, like, with things like GTM, the Google Tag Manager.
You know, it's relatively easy at times to set up those sorts of events.
But even then, that would still have – it's about weighing impact for effort of that type of work and, like, would it really help you uncover something?
And so, you know, folks like myself and who have a lot of experience around that is where we can provide the value and it's not that we can't necessarily.
It's just not usually, like, or our approach is not usually standing for analytics, you know.
I just didn't know if y'all would be that tactical.
Um.
Thank
What about, can you kind of give us an idea around cost and, you know, what your structure looks like?
Yeah.
And so, you know, this depends on a few different factors.
And so happy to talk about at some point, if applicable, you know, what that looks like and how costs can be, you know, adjusted based off your needs.

But essentially, strategy sprint, the way we structure this, as mentioned, six to eight weeks, this is where, you know, we're building a lot of those different artifacts, as mentioned, setting a roadmap, calibrating on content, starting to publish content as well.
There's no obligation to work with us beyond this phase, but the intention is to be able to set context for a long-term engagement.
What we find is this just far better enables, you know, our customers up for success, but also, you know, if this isn't a fit for you, these artifacts truly can be leveraged to get a lot of great.
Ultram AI content.
We have current customers in Sprint who are seeing that now and taking our artifacts and running it with other things they're doing and seeing some amazing results.
But that's not the intention of why we do these strategy sprints.
It's really to we want to find partners who are in the long term, who see the value of a long term engagement.
And this helps give a lot of context of what that would look like.
These strategy sprints typically range in 20 to 30K, depending on complexity.
And then for growth execution phase, where we're getting to daily publishing and what is actually needed within there, there could be a high degree of variance of what each customer needs.
You know, we have some that need to pass legal review, you know, medical review, et cetera.
And like, what does that bar look like that we need to hit?
Typical, you know, engagement will range from like 9K on the low end to sometimes mid 20s on the high end, you know, but more on average.
It's typically in like that 12 to 15K a month range for the growth execution phase.
And so this is where, you know, we are really starting to execute on that roadmap, continuing to evolve it, and then can later on different things as needed over time.
Okay.
Can you send us this deck?
We've all had to jump on another call right now.
But this was super helpful.
Thank you so much, Kyle.
Yeah, you got it.
I will send this over and let me know if it makes sense to have a follow-up.
Just as a quick, we tend to book up several weeks in advance.
so if there is interest, just noting that to have that in your mind.
great.
All right.
Thank you so much.
Yeah, take care.
Thank you, Tom.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Explore GrowthX's capabilities in content creation, SEO, and GEO optimization for Arkose Labs' potential vendor selection.](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX offers a hybrid AI workflow platform and service for content creation, focusing on deep business understanding and persona-driven content](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=9.0)
  - [Their approach emphasizes quality content at scale, starting with editorial content and expanding to programmatic, lead magnets, and other formats](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=641.0)
  - [GrowthX's process includes a 6-8 week strategy sprint ($20-30K) followed by ongoing growth execution ($9-25K/month, avg. $12-15K)](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1688.0)
  - [Measurement of GEO impact on conversions remains complex, requiring collaboration with the client's existing attribution systems](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=866.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX's Approach to SEO and GEO

  - [Focus on creating high-quality, engaging content that answers audience questions](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=334.0)
  - [Use of AI workflows to scale content production while maintaining quality](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=660.0)
  - [Emphasis on understanding business deeply, including personas and pain points](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=334.0)
  - [Tracking GEO performance using tools like Scrunch to monitor prompt presence](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=453.0)
  - [Balance between optimizing for search engines and creating valuable content for users](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=387.0)

### Content Creation Process

  - [6-8 week strategy sprint to develop deep insights and AI-ready artifacts](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1093.0)
  - [Creation of detailed personas, writing guidelines, and content structures](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1142.0)
  - [Use of AI workflows for efficient content generation, with human editing and oversight](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=666.0)
  - [Focus on editorial content initially, expanding to programmatic and other formats](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=641.0)
  - [Team includes experienced editors and strategists from major publications and brands](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1031.0)

### Measuring Impact and Conversions

  - [Challenges in directly attributing GEO impact to conversions](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=446.0)
  - [Suggestions for tracking:](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1540.0)
      - [Direct link clicks from GEO sources](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1500.0)
      - [Monitoring branded search and direct traffic increases](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1554.0)
      - [Considering incrementality measurements](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1562.0)
  - [GrowthX doesn't typically set up attribution systems but can advise on approach](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1613.0)

### Pricing and Engagement Structure

  - [Strategy sprint: $20-30K for 6-8 weeks](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1760.0)
  - [Ongoing growth execution: $9-25K/month (average $12-15K)](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1790.0)
  - [No obligation to continue after strategy sprint](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1718.0)
  - [Pricing varies based on complexity and specific client needs](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1760.0)

## Next Steps

  - [GrowthX to send the presentation deck to Arkose Labs team](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1817.0)
  - [Arkose Labs to review information and consider follow-up if interested](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1817.0)
  - [Note: GrowthX typically books several weeks in advance for new engagements](https://fathom.video/share/a3rNycpknhLgSPtygY8zxr5fe3HeeTks?tab=summary&timestamp=1833.0)

action_items.description: Review GrowthX deck (to be sent by Kyle); decide on follow-up meeting
